Bug.Description: Recursive fault on system check (critical or lower) due to poor DIAG X'288' hercules patch
Bug.Behavior: If any subsystem of the kernel triggers a system check (critical or lower), kernel will recursively fault with Operation Exception, and/or trigger a restart (due to misconfigured timebomb)
Bug.Solution: Patches Hercules DIAG X'288' Patch to be more robust (also mirror linux)

Bug.Description: IRQ descriptor table slot-number allocation mismatch causes all SCOMS-managed IRQ dispatch to silently fail, posting no handlers for any external interrupt (clock comparator, CPU timer, IPI, etc.)
Bug.Behavior:
irq_register() called g_irq_table.alloc() which pulled an arbitrary free-list slot index S, populating m_slots[S] with the handler. irq_dispatch() and find_raw() look up slots by the IRQ vector number V (so m_slots[V]), which remained with ko.id == KOBJECT_ID_INVALID. The lookup returned nullptr and the handler was never invoked.
Consequence: ext.cxxm routed every external interrupt through the IRQ table (instead of direct fast paths), so clock comparator events, CPU timer ticks, timing alerts, service signals, and IPI vectors all produced zero dispatch work. Without timer-driven scheduler ticks the BSP stopped advancing, and APs entering sched_init_ap simultaneously raced raw PMM allocations with no scheduler heartbeat, yielding nondeterministic PMM free-list corruption (assert_allocatable_frame with frame_owner::stack, order 2), deadlocks (254/255 progress stall), or undefined spin-wait hangs.
Bug.Solution:
Introduced kobject_table::alloc_at(u32 id) which claims a specific slot index by unlinking it from the SCOMS free-list rather than taking an arbitrary slot from the free-list head. irqdesc::irq_register() now calls alloc_at(irq) so the irq_desc for vector V always occupies m_slots[V], making find_raw(V) reach the correct descriptor.
Additional benefit: alloc_at() is robust against reuse of a slot by a different IRQ vector — if m_slots[id].ko.id != KOBJECT_ID_INVALID, the call returns already_locked, which replaces the silent double-registration that existed before.
